results.title: Adequacy of Existing Laws to Punish Egregious Road Behaviour That Result in Death
results.summary: The Minister clarified that existing Penal Code provisions cover deaths caused by vehicles, with charges ranging from murder (mandatory or discretionary death penalty) to culpable homicide (up to 15 years imprisonment) depending on the offender's state of mind. Most fatal driving cases are prosecuted under the Road Traffic Act because offenders typically lack the intent required for murder or culpable homicide charges. While the Ministry of Home Affairs has increased penalties and is considering new offences for driving that endangers life or causes death/grievous hurt, these proposed measures will not carry the death penalty. The Government has not introduced a specific vehicular homicide offence with penalties on par with culpable homicide, as doing so could lead to charges for murder even without intent to cause death.

results.title: Acceptance Rates of Revised HealthHub Terms of Use and Alternative Access Options
results.summary: HealthHub's Terms of Use were updated in September and November 2025 to comply with Data Protection Trustmark requirements and enhance the Caregiver Access module. The Minister reported that monthly unique user numbers have not declined following these updates. Residents who do not accept the revised terms or prefer not to use the platform can access equivalent healthcare information by contacting public healthcare institutions directly via their service counters and contact centres. No specific count of non-accepting users was provided.

results.title: AI Literacy Courses for Prison Inmates
results.summary: Yellow Ribbon Singapore and the Singapore Prison Service provide digital skills training aligned with job market demands, benefiting approximately 3,600 inmates since 2022. While structured AI literacy courses are under exploration, a specific pilot will trial in March 2026, equipping elderly inmates with basic knowledge of Generative AI tools in collaboration with IMDA's Seniors Go Digital programme. Additionally, YRSG will commence "SkillsFuture for Digital Workplace 2.0" training for community supervisees in 2026, which includes essential AI-related skills. The Ministry is actively considering partnerships with educational providers to deliver further AI literacy training within prison institutions.

results.title: Appropriate Assessment Models for Primary School Students to Balance Exam Preparation with Inquiry-based Learning and Well-being
results.summary: The Minister clarified that while schools employ inquiry-based learning and diverse school-based assessments like project work, the PSLE retains a distinct purpose as a curriculum checkpoint, placement guide, and objective benchmark for secondary school posting. He noted that any assessment form generates some stress, attributing academic pressure primarily to how results are used and performance expectations rather than the assessment mode itself. Consequently, MOE has already implemented measures such as blunting PSLE scoring and removing mid-year exams to reduce overemphasis on results. The Ministry will engage stakeholders during upcoming Education Conversations to address concerns regarding the education "arms race" and examination stakes, though major changes require careful consideration given differing global contexts.

results.title: Barrier-free Access on Public Footpaths for Mobility Scooters and Motorised Wheelchairs
results.summary: The Acting Minister for Transport stated that since 2018, all new standalone footpaths must have a minimum clear width of 1.8 metres, while those adjacent to cycling paths require at least 1.5 metres. Existing footpaths are widened to these standards during rejuvenation unless site constraints, such as lack of roadside space, exist. Widening works in private estates are typically undertaken alongside other programmes like drainage or estate improvements and are not solely within the Land Transport Authority's jurisdiction. Enforcement authorities will exercise discretion if riders must briefly use roads to bypass obstructions. The Government adopts a calibrated approach considering user volumes and practical constraints, noting that timelines for specific private estate upgrades depend on concurrent improvement works.

results.title: Breakdown of IPPT Performance for National Servicemen by Age Group in Past 10 Years
results.summary: Over the last decade, IPPT passing rates for National Servicemen have remained stable across all age groups. Among those attempting the test, approximately 35% achieved Gold or Silver awards, 35% received a Pass with Incentive, and 15% obtained a Pass. These percentages have been relatively consistent since 2017. The Minister noted that performance distribution is similar regardless of age, indicating no significant shift in overall physical proficiency standards for NSmen during this period.

results.title: Breakdown of Public Funds Disbursed to NTUC across Programmes and Grants for Past Five Years
results.summary: The Minister for Manpower stated that NTUC serves as a key service delivery partner for MOM, receiving funding for specific programmes aligned with policy objectives such as employment facilitation, the Career Conversion Programme, and the administration of Company Training Committees. A total of $300 million was set aside for the CTC Grant to support enterprise transformation, with portions potentially covering partner manpower costs. The Minister clarified that MOM does not track consolidated whole-of-Government funding for NTUC and explicitly does not provide dedicated funds for NTUC's general staffing or executive remuneration. Consequently, no consolidated figures for operational expenditure or executive pay were disclosed, as such data is not tracked across the government.

results.title: Committee of Supply Reporting Progress
results.summary: The Committee of Supply reported progress on the Estimates of Expenditure for the 2026/2027 financial year and requested leave to resume proceedings the following day. No specific expenditure figures, programme details, or policy measures were disclosed during this brief procedural update. The committee will continue its work on the supply estimates in the next session.

results.title: Committee of Supply – Head R (Ministry of Law)
results.summary: The Ministry of Law will explore increasing Wills Registry search fees to cover maintenance costs, while confirming that Attorney-General appointments remain a structured, non-partisan process requiring assessment beyond basic eligibility. Civil legal aid coverage expands this year, and the Land Betterment Charge regime will be enhanced to optimise state assets for economic and social needs. The Ministry continues legal reforms to keep laws fit for purpose amid disruptive forces like Artificial Intelligence, which will impact document review and contract drafting. Mandatory mediation and Community Disputes Resolution Unit services will expand beyond the Tampines pilot to improve community justice frameworks. Additionally, the government supports the legal profession's growth through technology adoption, emerging opportunities in ESG, and initiatives to empower the economy while maintaining a human-centric regime.

results.title: Connectivity Dead-zones and Weak Signal Strength in Bidadari Estate in Past Five Years and Measures to Address Connectivity Gaps
results.summary: The Ministry of Digital Development and Information (MDDI) confirmed that over the past five years, the Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) received eight complaints regarding weak mobile coverage in Bidadari Estate. Five of these issues have been resolved, while the remaining three concerns regarding the basement carpark of Alkaff Courtview Estate are being addressed by Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) within the first half of 2026. MNOs must comply with IMDA's Quality of Service standards, and the agency conducts regular island-wide checks to ensure adherence. The MNOs are collaborating with the Housing and Development Board (HDB) to facilitate network coverage in new towns, including Bidadari.

results.title: Data for Rentals of Privately-owned HDB Shops and for Licensing of Outdoor Refreshment Areas
results.summary: Privately-owned HDB shop rental data is aggregated by agencies like IRAS and published via the Urban Redevelopment Authority's Real Estate Information System to balance transparency with commercial sensitivities. HDB announced in January 2026 that it will collect stall rent data for HDB-owned coffeeshops to assist stallholders in decision-making, with future public availability under assessment. Additionally, HDB aims to publish Temporary Occupation Licence (TOL) data for outdoor refreshment areas to increase transparency on fees paid by operators. The Ministry does not mandate direct reporting of such data but relies on existing agency records for evidence-based assessment of rental pressures.

results.title: Committee of Supply – Head K (Ministry of Education)
results.summary: Minister Shawn Huang Wei Zhong addressed concerns regarding the Gifted Education Programme's discontinuation in 2027, affirming that its spirit of differentiated instruction will be preserved system-wide while embedding AI literacy across the entire learning life cycle. The Ministry outlined a progression from foundational cognitive competencies at the primary level to critical interrogation at the pre-university stage, ensuring AI tools deepen learning rather than serving as shortcuts. To counteract high-stakes exam pressures and the "education arms race," the government introduced "White Space Fridays" for self-directed exploration and proposed Student Policy Committees to foster inclusivity. While acknowledging Singapore's top PISA ranking, the Minister emphasized that current metrics capture only cognitive potential, neglecting affective domains where emotional disengagement impairs performance. Consequently, MOE is reviewing Primary 1 admission priorities to reduce anxiety and ensuring sustainable talent pathways for Direct School Admissions students.

results.title: Data for Time Taken for Retrenched Workers to Return to Workforce
results.summary: Based on a six-month post-retrenchment follow-up in 2025, the Ministry reported mean re-employment durations of 2.6 months for workers under 40, 2.7 months for those aged 40 to 59, and 2.2 months for those aged 60 and above. Median durations were 1 month for the 60-and-above group and 2 months for younger cohorts. These statistics quantify the time taken for retrenched residents to re-enter employment across different age brackets.

results.title: Data on Composition and Distribution of Household Income Changes Across Different Income Deciles
results.summary: In 2025, the average household market income per member for resident households in the fifth income decile rose by $287. This increase comprised $183 from employment income and $104 from non-employment sources. Detailed data on average rental and investment income per household member across all income deciles is available in Table 1. The Deputy Prime Minister and Minister for Trade and Industry provided these figures in response to a query regarding the composition of household income changes.

results.title: Data on Employment Assistance Payment Payouts Made to Retiring Workers for 2024 and 2025
results.summary: In 2024 and 2025, over 98% of resident workers reaching statutory retirement age were offered reemployment, with only 0.4% and 0.6% respectively receiving the Employment Assistance Payment (EAP) instead. The average EAP payout was $12,300 in 2024 and $17,400 in 2025. These figures reflect the proportion of retiring workers not offered reemployment and the corresponding financial assistance provided under the current framework.

results.title: Data on Employment Pass Holders in Statutory Boards and Government Agencies
results.summary: There are approximately 2,700 Employment Pass holders currently employed within Ministries and Statutory Boards. These individuals primarily occupy roles requiring skills in shortage among the local workforce, specifically in engineering and information technology. The Minister for Finance confirmed these figures and role classifications during the parliamentary session. No specific breakdown for Government agencies separate from Ministries was provided in the response.

results.title: Committee of Supply – Head Q (Ministry of Digital Development and Information)
results.summary: The Ministry of Digital Development and Information (MDDI) addressed scrutiny of its $100 budget reduction and the mandate of the National AI Council (NAIC), clarifying that the Council will actively drive societal AI transformation rather than serving solely as an advisory body. MDDI stated that while Public Order Management Act (POFMA) was not used against specific AI-generated misinformation, public education measures are the current response to fabricated domestic political claims, with a shift toward targeted, risk-based requirements for high-impact AI systems instead of one-size-fits-all regulations. The ministry outlined plans to develop sovereign AI compute capacity, strengthen cybersecurity against advanced persistent threats, and support SMEs through upfront de-risking incentives covering up to 90% of qualifying costs. Furthermore, MDDI emphasized that digital transformation success is defined by improved citizen experience and seamless service delivery, requiring a modular approach that balances speed, security, cost, and trust while ensuring workforce readiness through initiatives like TeSA.

results.title: Data on Financial Stress-related Mental Health Concerns Among Social Assistance Recipients in Past Three Years and Comprehensive Approach to Address Issues
results.summary: The Minister for Social and Family Development clarified that while Social Service Offices refer clients with known mental health concerns to community teams, applicants are not required to disclose mental health information during ComCare assessments to avoid deterring those in need. No data was provided regarding an increase in financial stress-related mental health concerns among recipients over the past three years. Consequently, specific interventions addressing the intersection of economic hardship and psychological well-being were not detailed in the response.

results.title: Data on Foreign Workers Deportation, Broken Down by Work Pass Category
results.summary: In 2025, the Immigration and Checkpoints Authority (ICA) deported approximately 2,500 foreign nationals holding Employment Passes, S Passes, or Work Permits under the Immigration Act 1959 for criminal offences or threats to public security. Of the 17 deportation appeals received, none were successful; however, the government declined to disclose specific appeal details or decision-making considerations. The Coordinating Minister for National Security and Minister for Home Affairs confirmed these figures while noting that deportation orders apply when continued presence is deemed undesirable or prejudicial to Singapore's public security.

results.title: Data on Motor Vehicles that Remain Unrectified Despite Vehicle Recalls
results.summary: From 2016 to 2025, approximately 500,000 vehicles registered in Singapore were affected by safety-related recalls initiated by manufacturers. Both manufacturers and dealers are legally obliged to notify owners, with enforcement actions taken against non-compliant dealers and owners. While most defects do not pose an imminent danger, owners are advised not to drive vehicles where a defect presents a serious safety risk until rectified. The Ministry does not currently propose suspending unrectified vehicles from road use but relies on manufacturer and dealer cooperation to identify and contact owners.

results.title: Defence Spending Strategy and Assessing Effective and Long-term Returns on Defence Investments
results.summary: Singapore's defence expenditure is guided by seven core principles prioritising operational needs, life cycle cost sustainability, integrated systems within the SAF, consistent long-term investment, resilience against disruptions, cost-effective "best solution" assessments, and unwavering focus on warfighter requirements. The Minister stated that decisions avoid non-operational distractions to ensure capabilities are built ahead of time rather than just-in-time. While specific frameworks or indicators for assessing effectiveness and long-term returns on investments, including tangible and intangible outcomes like deterrence, were not detailed in the response, the stated principles serve as the underpinning considerations for all spending and procurement. The approach emphasises steady investment in technology and skills while maintaining alternative supply sources and developing customised systems where necessary to meet unique national needs.

results.title: Energy Consumption Projections of AI Park at one-north and Energy Efficiency Measures
results.summary: The AI park at one-north is designed as a developer hub rather than a data centre park, with projected annual electricity and water consumption expected to match other technology-intensive industrial estates like the Punggol Digital District. Under the National AI Research and Development plan, resource-efficient AI is a research priority, though no internationally recognised standard for software-level energy efficiency currently exists. The Infocomm Media Development Authority (IMDA) is collaborating with the Green Software Foundation on the methodology for the new Software Carbon Intensity International Organization for Standardization standard. In the interim, IMDA promotes efficient computing through the Green Software Trials and the Practical Green Software Guide for Enterprises, while the Ministry of Digital Development and Innovation is developing baseline energy efficiency standards for data centres under the Digital Infrastructure Act.

results.title: Ensuring IHL Students from Disadvantaged Backgrounds Have Equitable Access to Internships
results.summary: Institutes of Higher Learning (IHLs) collaborate with companies and sector agencies to provide diverse internship opportunities to all students regardless of background. To support graduation requirements, IHLs offer resume coaching, interview skills training, and direct intervention to match students with suitable placements. These measures ensure that students needing an internship to graduate can secure one and meet their academic obligations. The Minister confirmed these active support mechanisms are currently in place to facilitate equitable access.

results.title: Expanding Services Offered by Neighbourhood GPs under HealthierSG to Reduce Hospital Workload
results.summary: The Minister clarified that neighbourhood GPs under HealthierSG already perform routine blood tests for patients and enrollees. While healthcare clusters collaborate to enhance referrals from public hospitals to partner GPs or polyclinics for follow-up care, the Ministry has not indicated plans to expand GP services to include simple procedures requested by hospitals. Consequently, the specific measure of allowing GPs to perform hospital-requested procedures to reduce hospital visits and transport costs for seniors remains unconfirmed in this response.

results.title: Committee of Supply – Head V (Ministry of Trade and Industry)
results.summary: The Ministry of Trade and Industry (MTI) affirmed its commitment to supporting enterprises in adapting to global uncertainties, technological disruptions, and energy constraints while maintaining a pragmatic balance between sustainability, security, and affordability. The Government will not pursue decarbonisation "at all costs," noting that solar can supply at best 10% of future electricity needs due to land and climate constraints, while no final decision has been made on advanced nuclear energy. To strengthen the ecosystem, MTI will streamline regulatory processes, rejuvenate heartland shops, and expand financing access via the Startup SG Equity and Anchor Funds. Regarding consumer protection, a review panel was convened in March to address deceptive practices, though specific findings are awaited. The Government stated there is no single trigger for responses to scenarios like tariff increases or energy cost spikes, with assessments made holistically instead.

results.title: Expansion of Singapore Sports School Diploma Programme for Student-Athletes
results.summary: The Singapore Sports School (SSP) has signed a Memorandum of Understanding with Temasek Polytechnic to offer five new STEM-based diploma programmes under its School of Applied Science, available for enrolment in 2027. This marks the first time SSP provides off-campus academic options, expanding its current arrangements to three out of five polytechnics offering diplomas in business and applied sciences. Additionally, SSP continues to offer the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme with flexibility for high-performing student-athletes to extend the duration to three years. The Ministry of Culture, Community and Youth and SSP will continue collaborating with education partners to create more athlete-friendly pathways.

results.title: Factors Determining Location and Establishment of ServiceSG Centres, Services with High Usage Frequency and Projection of New Centres from 2026 to 2028
results.summary: ServiceSG Centres provide face-to-face support for approximately 600 services across 25 agencies, targeting citizens needing assistance with digital transactions. High-frequency services include Singpass account management, CPF nominations, and HDB rental or carpark applications. Since 2021, 10 centres have been established islandwide. Future rollout from 2026 to 2028 will progressively expand the number of centres based on population density, demographics, and areas less well-served by digital or physical Government services. The expansion aims to support growing populations requiring additional digital support while maintaining fiscal prudence. Specific projected numbers and exact locations for new centres between 2026 and 2028 were not detailed in the response.

results.title: Faster Response Targets for LTA Online Contact Form in Comparison with other Government Benchmarks and Standards across Agencies
results.summary: The Acting Minister for Transport stated that Government agencies, including the Land Transport Authority (LTA), are expected to reply within 15 working days. LTA reports that over 60% of feedback is resolved within five working days, while more than 93% are closed within 14 working days. For complex cases exceeding this timeframe, LTA acknowledges feedback within 15 days and keeps providers informed of progress. The Minister did not confirm a review of the 14 to 20-day standard for complex cases mentioned by Mr David Hoe.

results.title: Funding Allocated to and Annual Operating and Construction Costs of NESST Singapore Limited for Dormitory Operations
results.summary: The Ministry of Manpower (MOM) owns the Tukang Purpose-Built Dormitory (Tukang PBD) with a total construction cost of $58 million. Under a Government Build-Own-Lease arrangement, NESST Singapore Limited operates the facility and pays monthly lease fees based on market valuation. Since its establishment in March 2023, MOM has provided approximately $6.2 million in funding to NESST for setup, pilot innovations, and capability growth. The Minister declined to disclose specific annual operating costs, citing commercial sensitivity regarding NESST's financial data.

results.title: Future Strategic Direction and Support Level for Cultivated Food Firms Given Recent Exits of Several Firms
results.summary: The Minister for Sustainability and the Environment directed Members to refer to the written reply provided on 26 February 2026 regarding the Government's support and strategy for the alternative protein sector. This written response addresses the assessment of reasons for recent cultivated food firm exits and outlines the Ministry's future strategic direction and support levels for the sector within Singapore's food security journey. No new details were presented during this debate session.

results.title: Governance of Student Care Centres and Minimising Disruptions in Event of Abrupt Centre Closures
results.summary: Following the abrupt closure of Little Professors Learning Centre Pte Ltd in February 2026, the Ministry of Education (MOE) deployed school staff on a rotational basis and engaged relief manpower to ensure uninterrupted student care. While affected teachers will be recognised for their extra efforts, MOE is currently sourcing new operators and employing former staff to provide interim stability. Regarding governance, since January 2025, all contracts require yearly audited financial statements, and operators must demonstrate adequate financial standing during selection. MOE is reviewing its monitoring mechanisms to strengthen operator resilience and is investigating claims for unpaid salaries and double GIRO deductions, advising parents to file claims with relevant agencies while police investigations proceed.

results.title: Growth in Real Wages of Lowest 20th Percentile of Workers Compared with Increase in Living Costs
results.summary: From 2022 to 2025, nominal wages for full-time employed residents at the 20th percentile rose cumulatively by 13.9%. While headline inflation reached 8.3% and core inflation was 7.8% over the same period, lower-wage workers at this percentile experienced a cumulative real wage growth of 5.2% after adjusting for headline inflation. The Ministry for Manpower provided these specific figures to assess the comparison between wage growth and essential living cost increases for the lowest 20th percentile of local workers.

results.title: Guidelines on School Admissions Policy Between Affiliated and Non-Affiliated Students
results.summary: Affiliated secondary schools may prioritize affiliated students provided they meet Affiliate Minimum Requirements (AMR) and reserve at least 20% of places in each Posting Group for non-affiliated students. The Ministry of Education does not cap the score difference between the Cut-Off Point for non-affiliated students and the AMR, as the former is determined by demand and choice patterns. For junior college admissions, affiliated students receive bonus points when selecting their affiliated institution as a first choice, with a maximum of four points applied to the calculation of net First Language and Relevant Subject scores. The Cut-Off Point for junior colleges remains determined by demand and choice patterns, reflecting the score of the last student posted to the school.

results.title: Implementation of Differentiated, Age-tiered Payout Ratios under Workfare Income Supplement Scheme
results.summary: The Minister explained that the Workfare Income Supplement (WIS) maintains a 60% CPF and 40% cash payout ratio for workers aged 70 and above to simultaneously build retirement savings and meet immediate needs, noting that CPF funds in the MediSave Account directly support medical expenses. While the cash-to-CPF ratio remains consistent across age bands, the Ministry of Manpower enhanced WIS payments from 2025, increasing annual cash for a 70-year-old earning $1,700 from $1,680 to $1,960. The Minister further highlighted additional Budget 2026 support measures, including up to $570 in U-Save rebates for HDB households, $200 to $400 Cost-of-Living Special Payments, and $500 Community Development Council Vouchers to address broader cost-of-living concerns.

results.title: Key Performance Indicators for Upcoming National Space Agency
results.summary: The National Space Agency of Singapore (NSAS) will be established on 1 April 2026. Over the next five years, NSAS will develop space technology capabilities to support national needs, including urban planning and port operations. The agency will collaborate with the research ecosystem to support Singapore companies in capturing opportunities in the growing space economy, covering advanced Earth observation, communications satellite technologies, geospatial applications, and sustainability-related technologies. Additionally, NSAS will build space situational awareness capabilities to ensure the safety of Singapore's space assets and formulate national space legislation and policies. Specific key performance indicators for the agency's first three to five years were not stated in the transcript.

results.title: Measures, Penalties and Enforcement Contained in Upcoming Regulations of Sale of Blind Boxes
results.summary: The Ministry of Home Affairs will introduce blind box regulations around mid-2026 to mitigate gambling-related risks and excessive spending. Initial measures will focus on core controls, with additional provisions like age-based restrictions and probability disclosure to be studied and considered subsequently. Specific details regarding implementation timelines, enforcement mechanisms, and penalties for non-compliance will be shared by the Ministry when ready. The response indicates that comprehensive measures addressing compulsive behavior among minors are under study rather than immediately finalized.

results.title: National Service Participation of Madrasah Students
results.summary: Since 2020, an average of 30 madrasah students per year have been enlisted for National Service. These students are represented across the SAF, SCDF, and SPF, subject to operational and manpower requirements. The Ministry of Defence assesses each deferment application for religious studies or leadership roles on a case-by-case basis. The transcript does not specify the exact percentage of eligible students who have not received Enlistment Notices or the precise distribution of enlisted students among the three uniformed services.

results.title: Noise Pollution Enforcement on Foreign-registered Vehicles and Data on Noise-related Summons Issued at Land Checkpoints in Past Three Years
results.summary: The Minister for Sustainability and the Environment stated that the specific count of noise emission fines among the 126,820 vehicular fines issued to foreign-registered vehicles in 2025, along with year-on-year percentage changes in noise-related summonses at land checkpoints over the past three years, has already been addressed in the reply to Parliamentary Questions Nos 18 and 19 at the 2 March 2026 Parliament Sitting. No new data or figures were provided during this session.

results.title: Outreach Efforts to Remind Foreign Drivers of Singapore's Vehicle Noise Emission Standards
results.summary: The National Environment Agency (NEA) reminds all motorists, including foreign-registered vehicles and regular cross-border commuters, to comply with Singapore's vehicular noise emission limits via media channels and posters at land entry checkpoints. Foreign drivers remain responsible for familiarising themselves with regulations before entry. Enforcement intensified in 2025, resulting in 217 fines for foreign vehicles, a significant increase from 106 in 2024 and 154 in 2023, attributed to wider coverage and on-site testing at checkpoints. While NEA explored acoustic cameras for enforcement, they were deemed unreliable for attributing noise sources in crowded traffic. The agency will continue exploring technologies to facilitate enforcement against noisy vehicles. The Ministry did not confirm plans to incorporate noise compliance reminders into Vehicle Entry Permit or Autopass application processes.

results.title: Proportion of SEN Students in Mainstream Schools and Ensuring Adequate Training for Teachers
results.summary: Approximately 7% of students in mainstream schools have reported special educational needs (SEN), a proportion that has remained stable over the past three years. The Ministry employs a whole-school support ecosystem comprising school leaders, Case Management Teams, Teachers trained in Special Needs (TSNs), and SEN Officers to address diverse learning needs. Specific interventions include the Transition Support for InTegration (TRANSIT) programme, which places Primary 1 students with social or behavioural needs in small classes of up to 10, and the School-based Dyslexia Remediation (SDR) Programme, offering explicit instruction to Primary 3 and 4 students in classes of four to six. Additionally, SEN Officers provide targeted behavioural and learning support in primary schools, with deployment levels adjusted based on the number of SEN students at each institution.

results.title: Publication of Advertising Code of Conduct for Tuition Centres and Accompanying Enforcement Actions
results.summary: The Minister for Education stated that the advertising code of conduct for tuition centres and accompanying enforcement actions have already been addressed in the answer to Oral Parliamentary Question No 1 on 24 February 2026. No new timeline, specific guidelines, or additional enforcement details were provided in this session. The matter is considered resolved based on the prior response.

results.title: Regulatory Framework for and Quality Assurance of Counselling Services
results.summary: Counsellors operate across health, education, and social service sectors with competencies defined by the Skills Framework for Social Service and the National Mental Health Competency Framework. Professional standards are upheld through institutional oversight in public healthcare, mandatory hiring requirements, clinical supervision, and continuous training for Ministry of Social and Family-funded programmes. The Singapore Association of Counselling provides voluntary accreditation via professional certification, a code of ethics, and ongoing support. The Ministry of Health adopts a risk-based regulatory approach, regularly assessing evolving risks in healthcare and mental health services to consider additional safeguards where necessary. No specific new regulatory framework or sanctions for unprofessional conduct were announced during the session.

results.title: Reliability and Extent of Use of Pulau Ubin's Electricity Microgrid
results.summary: In 2025, Pulau Ubin's microgrid served over 30 consumers with ~300 MWh annual consumption, where solar met 10% of demand and diesel covered the rest. An experimental vanadium flow battery is operational, with an upgraded system expected later this year to increase solar utilisation. The system recorded 13 power supply trips in 2025. Operator EDPR is upgrading the system to enhance reliability. Affected users may make representations to EDPR for compensation, and all previous claims have been settled.

results.title: Restitution for Motorists Who Might Have Had Driving Licences Suspended Due To Recent Incident of Erroneous Issuance of Speeding Tickets
results.summary: The Coordinating Minister for National Security and Minister for Home Affairs directed Members to refer to a written reply delivered on 25 February 2026 regarding restitution for motorists whose licences were suspended due to 1,523 erroneously issued speeding tickets between 30 October and 8 December 2025. No specific restitution measures, timelines, or eligibility criteria were stated in the parliamentary record. The Government's operational response to the wrongful loss of driving privileges remains unclarified in this transcript.

results.title: Review of Childcare Leave Allocation for Children Aged Seven to 12
results.summary: The Government will consider reviewing childcare leave provisions for children aged seven to twelve, currently limited to two days annually compared to six days for younger children. This review will account for broader parental caregiving responsibilities and health needs. The Minister stated that younger children are generally less independent, justifying the current disparity. The suggestion to adjust leave allocation for older children will be taken into account during the ongoing review of current leave provisions.

results.title: Reviewing Fee Structure and Possible Tiered Service Options for Day Activity Centres
results.summary: The Minister clarified that Day Activity Centre fees are tiered based on per capita household income rather than a binary structure, with clients eligible for part-time enrolment and pro-rated out-of-pocket payments. This existing arrangement allows middle-income households to access services without requiring new fiscal commitments or expanded capacity. Consequently, the Ministry has not assessed pricing out effects or proposed additional tiered options, as the current flexible payment scales already address accessibility concerns.

results.title: Risk Management and Corporate Accountability of Companies Receiving EDB Incentives
results.summary: EDB incentives are awarded to the Singapore entity undertaking substantive economic activities, legally binding it to specified commitments with clawback provisions for non-compliance. The Deputy Prime Minister clarified that personnel rotations do not affect these legal obligations, ensuring continuity of commitments without requiring sign-off from regional or global headquarters. The Ministry did not state whether it will consider requiring a global headquarters interlocutor as a condition for incentive approval to prevent orphaned projects.

results.title: Safeguarding Healthcare Affordability On Top of New Requirements for MediShield IP Riders
results.summary: The Ministry for Health announced new Integrated Shield Plan (IP) rider requirements effective 26 November 2025, including increased minimum co-payments and disallowing coverage for minimum IP deductibles to moderate private healthcare cost escalation. Additional measures under study include developing claim rules, enforcing against inappropriate claims, publishing hospital charge benchmarks, and enhancing public insurance education. The Minister emphasized that while these steps manage costs, the most impactful affordability safeguards remain in healthcare financing through enhanced subsidies and MediShield Life coverage. Ultimately, all financing methods translate into taxes, premiums, or out-of-pocket payments borne by the population, requiring sustainable long-term policy design.

results.title: Singapore's Vaccine Safety and Efficacy Validation Process, and Submission for Moderna’s mRNA Influenza Vaccine
results.summary: The Health Sciences Authority (HSA) confirmed it has not received a regulatory submission for Moderna's mRNA seasonal influenza vaccine. HSA maintains independent regulatory authority, utilizing a science-based expert panel to assess submissions without simply aligning with US or UK regulators. The review process evaluates comprehensive data from Phase 3 randomised controlled trials, ensuring adequate sample sizes and follow-up periods of at least six months to detect post-launch side effects. Manufacturers must also demonstrate compliance with good manufacturing practice (GMP) standards and provide stability data proving product effectiveness throughout storage. This robust system ensures approvals are based on safety and efficacy specific to Singapore's population needs.

results.title: Smart Public Outdoor Fitness Corners for Seniors and Lower-Income Residents in Heartland Areas
results.summary: The Health Promotion Board (HPB) confirmed it has not assessed the potential for "smart" public outdoor fitness corners with guided workouts. Instead, Singapore relies on existing infrastructure comprising over 3,400 outdoor fitness corners and 28 ActiveSG gyms island-wide to serve seniors and lower-income residents. HPB supports facility usage through HealthHub online resources and the Healthy 365 app, which allows residents to track activities, set goals, and access digital physical activity programmes. No pilot projects for smart facilities in heartland areas such as Clementi were proposed.

results.title: Support for Children At Risk of Developmental Delays and Behavioural Issues Due To Ill Treatment by Caregivers
results.summary: The Ministry of Social and Family Development does not track the number of children under four at risk of developmental delays or behavioural issues due to caregiver ill treatment. The Anchor Programme, piloted at KK Women's and Children's Hospital, is not geographically bound and serves children from across Singapore. While the Protective Service coordinates statutory interventions with the hospital, the Ministry welcomes other hospitals to consider similar programmes if a need is identified among their patients.

results.title: Take-up Rate of LPA Adoption by Age Group and Effectiveness of Public Outreach Programmes
results.summary: As of January 2026, 21% of Singapore citizens aged 50 and above have made a Lasting Power of Attorney (LPA), with 74% of these individuals falling between 50 and 75 years old. Digital campaigns effectively engage those aged 30 to 49, while traditional media raises awareness for those 50 and older. To sustain uptake after the registration fee waiver ends, the Ministry of Social and Family Development is expanding collaborations with financial institutions and community organisations. Additionally, the ministry is sending curated messages to nudge individuals toward making their LPA early.

results.title: Threat Assessment of Coordinated Deepfake or Synthetic Media Campaigns that Target Singapore Given Advances in GenAI
results.summary: The Government acknowledges that generative AI advances increase risks of coordinated deepfake campaigns targeting Singapore. Safeguards include the Protection from Online Falsehoods and Manipulation Act to correct AI-generated falsehoods, the Broadcasting Act enabling the Infocomm Media Development Authority to block egregious AI content, and elections laws prohibiting candidate-misrepresenting deepfakes. The Foreign Interference (Countermeasures) Act empowers blocking of hostile AI content. Detection capabilities utilize commercial and in-house tools, supported by the Centre for Advanced Technologies in Online Safety. Ahead of the 2025 General Elections, the Ministry of Home Affairs launched a public education campaign on deepfake influence operations, complemented by the National Library Board's S.U.R.E. programme to foster citizen vigilance.

results.title: Tiered Foreign Manpower and Levy Thresholds for Hawkers and Small F&B Operators
results.summary: The Minister for Manpower stated that differentiated foreign manpower thresholds for hawkers and small F&B operators are not currently feasible, as such measures could incentivise larger operators to fragment into smaller entities to hire more foreign workers, which would be unsustainable given Singapore's limited carrying capacity. While the Ministry of Sustainability and the Environment restricts hawker centre stallholders to Citizens and Permanent Residents, licensed food shops may hire foreign workers subject to levy rates and a two-to-one local-to-foreigner quota, with micro-enterprises allowed to hire their first Work Permit Holder despite having only one local worker. The Government emphasises that productivity, not headcount, must drive business viability, offering support through the FoodX programme, F&B Process Optimisation Programme, and Productivity Solutions Grant to improve operational efficiency and outsource labour-intensive tasks. The Ministry will continue reviewing policies alongside relevant agencies, noting that increasing headcount could impact recipe authenticity and that smaller operators must rethink their business models to remain sustainable.

results.title: Time Taken for Adult Caregivers to Return to Workforce After Discharging Caregiving Duties
results.summary: The Ministry of Manpower does not track the specific duration for adult caregivers to return to the workforce after discharging duties, as caregiving arrangements often evolve and individuals may work while still caring. Available data indicates that approximately one in four residents who were outside the labour force primarily due to caregiving returned to work within two years. Among those who did not return, around eight in ten had no intention to seek employment. The Ministry will continue monitoring labour force participation patterns among caregivers and make adjustments where appropriate.

results.title: Total Salaries of Political Office Holders Seconded to NTUC in Last Five Years
results.summary: The Government seconds political office holders to NTUC to strengthen partnership and shape labour policies. Under the 2012 White Paper, these appointees receive a single pay package as appointment holders, with costs shared between the Government and NTUC. The Minister did not disclose the total clean wage equivalent amount attributable to these salaries over the last five years.

results.title: Tuberculosis Screening for Long-term Pass Holders and Frequent Cross-border Travellers
results.summary: The Ministry maintains a risk-based approach for long-term pass holders, requiring chest X-rays for active TB screening upon arrival and during renewal, while Employment Pass applicants submit medical declarations due to low prevalence. Short-term visitor screening is deemed impractical as it would deter tourism. All TB cases are notifiable under the Infectious Diseases Act and subject to mandatory treatment, with infants protected by the Bacillus Calmette-Guérin vaccine against meningitis for 10 to 15 years. The Communicable Diseases Agency will continue assessing potential enhancements to screening measures alongside relevant agencies based on global and local epidemiological situations.

results.title: Update on Rectification Status of Singaporean Vehicles Affected by Defective Takata Airbag
results.summary: As of February 2026, approximately 215,000 vehicles in Singapore are affected by the Takata airbag recall. The Land Transport Authority reports that over 90% of these units have been rectified, with roughly 16,000 vehicles currently undergoing rectification. This leaves a small number of affected vehicles yet to be addressed. The Acting Minister for Transport provided these latest figures during the parliamentary session.

results.title: Update on Singapore Youth League
results.summary: The Singapore Youth League (SYL) launched in 2024 now involves 6,000 participants who play an average of 30 competitive matches annually, double the pre-SYL volume. Acting Minister for Culture, Community and Youth David Neo stated that national youth development relies on a whole-of-nation effort, integrating private academies, clubs, and the Singapore Premier League to build a broad talent base. The Ministry works with these ecosystem stakeholders to identify talents for the Junior National Development Centre and National Development Centre.

results.title: Updates on Non-unionised Employee Representation by Non-Governmental Organisations at Employment Claims Tribunal
results.summary: The Ministry for Manpower confirmed that the Employment Claims Tribunals maintains a simplified, guided process accessible to non-unionised employees without legal representation. Workers may apply for a Community Courts Tribunals Friend, allowing a third party from a non-governmental organisation to attend proceedings and provide support. This measure aims to assist parties lacking legal counsel while the tribunal retains its accessible framework. No specific timeline for further changes to representation rules was provided in the response.

results.title: Usage of Self-testing Emissions Facility and Participation Incentives for Motorcyclists
results.summary: The National Environment Agency conducted a three-month trial of the motorcycle emissions self-testing facility at Woodlands Checkpoint from November 2023 to February 2024. Riders were randomly selected and guided by on-site instructions, with NEA officers monitoring procedures to ensure accuracy and prevent tampering. Approximately 700 motorcycles were tested, resulting in enforcement actions against roughly 230 that exceeded exhaust emission limits. The agency is now exploring enhancements to improve the facility's usability and scalability for future implementation.
